content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

“Agent = LLM + tools + loop”. That is the definition of an agent everyone repeats, and I could recite it too, but I had never actually read the code that runs it. So I read one: Hugging Face’s smolagents, whose entire agent lives in one file, agents.py. I will trace the loop through the three functions that make it work, then say why my AI cohort’s Pydantic AI lab felt so different.
What does the loop actually look like?

• The driver _run_stream. This is the loop itself:
self.step_number = 1
returned_final_answer = False
while not returned_final_answer and self.step_number <= max_steps:
# ...planning step + action-step setup...
for output in self._step_stream(action_step):
yield output
if isinstance(output, ActionOutput) and output.is_final_answer:
returned_final_answer = True
self.memory.steps.append(action_step) # every step goes into memory
self.step_number += 1It keeps taking steps until the agent gives a final answer or hits max_steps , the safety valve that stops it looping forever. It does not do the work itself: each turn it calls _step_stream, then saves the finished step to memory.
It also splits failures in two. If my code is broken (AgentGenerationError), it crashes. If the model messed up (AgentError), it does not crash . It saves the error onto the step and keeps going. That saved error is the key to the self-correction trick further down.
2. One Step _step_stream. This is the single Thought -> Action:
memory_messages = self.write_memory_to_messages() # rebuild the prompt# call the model: give it the tools, and stop it before it can fake an observation
chat_message = self.model.generate(
memory_messages,
stop_sequences=["Observation:", "Calling tools:"],
tools_to_call_from=self.tools_and_managed_agents,
)
# run whatever tool calls the model returned
for output in self.process_tool_calls(chat_message, memory_step):
yield output
yield ActionOutput(output=final_answer, is_final_answer=got_final_answer)
Each step rebuilds the prompt with write_memory_to_messages(), then calls the model , handing it the tools it can use and stopping it at "Observation:" so it can't fake a result and has to let a real tool run. Then it runs whatever tools the model asked for and saves the results. final_answer is just another tool: when the model calls it, the step marks itself done and tells _run_stream to end the loop.
3. Memory ->prompt ->write_memory_to_messages. The bridge, and it's three lines:
messages = self.memory.system_prompt.to_messages()
for memory_step in self.memory.steps:
messages.extend(memory_step.to_messages())
return messagesA model has no memory of its own. So every turn, the agent rebuilds the whole conversation from memory.steps: system prompt, then the task, then every past action and observation. "Context" is a list you read out loud again each turn.
Here’s how the three connect, which is the real point: _run_stream loops. Each turn it calls _step_stream, which calls write_memory_to_messages to see everything so far, then acts and the result (or the error) gets added back into memory.steps. Memory is the only thing carried from one turn to the next.
_run_stream → _step_stream → write_memory_to_messages → act → result/error back into memory.steps → repeat
That’s what closes the loop on the error from before. When a step fails, _run_stream saves the error to memory. So next turn, the model reads "you tried X, it failed with Y" and fixes it. The self-correcting-agent magic is just: catch the error, add it to memory, let the loop come back around. The wizard is a try/except and a list.
Why the lab felt like magic?
My cohort’s lab does all of this too ,but on PydanticAI, which runs the loop for you. You supply a model, a system prompt, a typed output, and four tools, call agent.run(...), and a validated answer comes out. The loop, the memory rebuild, the retry -all of it happens out of sight. Same three functions, sealed inside the framework.
Why seeing it mattered?
I learned to drive on a manual in India; I drive an automatic in the US now. The automatic is easier and I am not going back -but I understand what it’s doing when it shifts because I once worked the clutch myself.
That’s these two. smolagents is the manual: I traced the loop, the memory rebuild, the error feeding back in. PydanticAI is the automatic I’ll happily drive from here -now knowing what the gearbox is doing when it shifts for me.
The lab was the magic show. smolagents is the same trick with a glass box. You don’t learn the trick by watching the show-you learn it by seeing the box.
